title: What is Beamstrike?
content:
Beamstrike is a miniature wargame set in the far future. The main 'playing piece' is the infantryman or trooper — your alter ego on the battlefield. Troops are classified firstly by the level of protection they have (armour) and secondly by the level of training they possess.

Beamstrike is divided into 5 sections:
- **Section 1** — Introduction
- **Section 2** — The Rules (core combat, movement, morale)
- **Section 3** — Vehicles
- **Section 4** — Home Manufacturing / Battles Available
- **Section 5** — Background (the Beamstrike universe)

title: Points Values and Army Lists
content:
Armies in Beamstrike are built to an agreed points limit. Each troop type, weapon, and piece of equipment has a points cost. The total points of all squads, weapons, and equipment in your army must not exceed the agreed points limit.

**Points are separate from Morale Value.** Points are used for army building. Morale value is tracked during the game:
- Each trooper = 1 morale point
- Each armoured vehicle or hero = 3 morale points
- Nominated objectives = 5–20 points (agreed before battle)

title: Armour Types
content:
Troops are classified by their armour level. Armour determines which column of the Infantry Damage Table is used when the figure is hit.

| Abbreviation | Description |
|---|---|
| **UA** | Unarmoured — no armour, only regular clothing. Civilians, low-tech troops, freedom-fighters. |
| **FI** | Fast Infantry — light ballistic armour. Able to move quickly. Scout troops and local law enforcement. |
| **LA** | Light Armoured — full suits of metallic or ballistic reactive composite armour with full helmets. Integrated breathing apparatus. |
| **PA** | Power Armoured — powered exo-skeletal structure. Heightened close combat ability and increased heavy weapon carrying allowance. |
| **AD** | Assault Dreadnought — the heaviest armour. Thick armour plated powered suit. Immune to Low and Standard power weapons. |

**NOTE ON ARMOUR:** Powered and Assault Dreadnought armour are both heavy and large. AD wearing troops can pack a real punch on the battlefield but are a little slow.

title: Training Levels
content:
Troop training level impacts small arms fire, hand to hand combat, weapon maintenance, and many other aspects of the rules. Most troops in organised armies are 'Regular' category.

| Training | Abbreviation | Troop Roll | Description |
|---|---|---|---|
| Civilian | CIV | 6 | Individuals with no combat training |
| Regular | REG | 5+ | Average, trained troops with limited battlefield experience |
| Veteran | VET | 4+ | Battle-hardened, experienced, well-trained troops |
| Elite | ELI | 3+ | Troops trained to a high level, including advanced weaponry and combat techniques |
| Hero | HER | 2+ | [Heroes section](#deployment-heroes) |

**Higher training levels cost more points per figure.**

title: Heroes
content:
Heroes are particularly capable individuals with multiple skills and bonuses in combat. They are worth taking time over, fleshing out a background story with motivations and goals.

**Key Hero rules:**
- Heroes may fire a pistol in each hand (ambidextrous firing)
- Heroes are allocated multiple skills according to the heroes section
- If a hero is 'killed', they are just removed from action for that battle — not permanently dead
- Heroes cost significant points due to their high skills and hit bonuses
- A MINIMUM of one leader per army MUST be chosen

Heroes with **Leadership skill** give several bonuses throughout the rules: units with a leader may engage targets other than the nearest, have a better chance of calling off-table fire support, and can rally an army's morale.

title: Troop Skills
content:
Skills are special attributes available to troops, each with a points cost. Track which figures have been given specific characteristics by means of distinctive colours or counters.

**Number of Skills Allowed:**
- Civilian: may not choose any skills
- Regular and Veteran: may have 1 skill
- Elite: may have 2 skills maximum
- Heroes: allocated multiple skills

| Skill | Points | Effect |
|---|---|---|
| **Leadership** | 8 pts/figure | Unit may engage non-nearest targets, +2 to suppression roll when in squad, better fire support calls. Min 1 per army. |
| **Sniper** | 3 pts | +enhanced fire/concealment bonuses when deployed. May 'pick off' enemy leaders. Max 3 per army. Only snipers may use the Sniper Laser (SL/SNR/HSR). |
| **Sapper** | 2 pts | Skilled at demolitions and destroying fortifications. May lay minefields, deactivate booby traps, and destroy enemy defense systems. |
| **Grenadier** | 2 pts | +2 to hit with thrown grenades, +1 to see if troops in cover are injured by exploding grenades, +1 H2H. Any number may be fielded. |
| **Medic** | 3 pts | May 'heal' one fallen comrade per turn. Up to 3 per army. Includes medical equipment. |
| **Fanatic** | 3 pts | Generally restricted to Rebel troops, some Aliens, and war droids. Do not retreat even when the army's morale is broken. |
| **Gunner** | 1 pt | May fire tripod-mounted light artillery and heavy weapons list without penalty. |
| **Scout** | 3 pts | Extra training in jet pack jumping (+3 inches), laser painting, and concealment. Up to 2 squads per army. |
| **Swordsman** | 3 pts | Martial arts specialist. +4 to 1D10 in H2H combat. Some melee weapons may ONLY be used with Swordsman skill. |

title: Equipping Troops
content:
There are sensible logistic limits on how many weapons/pieces of equipment each trooper may carry. These limits MUST not be exceeded.

| Troop Armour | Hand weapon | Pistol | Main weapon | Equipment pieces |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| UA | 1 | 1 | 1 | 1 |
| FI | 1 | 2 | 1 | 3 |
| LA | 1 | 2 | 1 | 3 |
| PA | 1 | 2 | 3 | 3 |
| AD | 1 | 0 | 4 | 4 |

- **Hand weapon:** any hand to hand melee weapon (heroes may carry 2 hand weapons)
- **Pistol:** any pistol weapon (inc. Palm Flamer) or grenade pack from the infantry weapons list
- **Main weapon:** any weapon carried by troops in Infantry weapons list or squad support weapon list (including pistols and grenade packs)
- **Equipment:** Digimedics, targeters, laser painters, shields, etc.

**PA/AD Weapon Packs:** PA and AD armoured troops may be given one main weapon as other troop classes, or have weapons installed in large arm 'packs'. Weapons are usually carried in 2 packs, one on each arm. Targeters fitted must state which weapon pack they are attached to.

title: Sequence of Play
content:
The sequence of play in Beamstrike is determined by an ordered manner according to the Play Sequence Chart. The battle is divided into a number of GAME TURNS, each consisting of 2 PLAYER TURNS. Within each PLAYER TURN there are several PHASES.

**Note:** There are two 'movement' phases per player turn and a firing phase in which BOTH players may fire simultaneously, casualties only being removed after both players have fired with all eligible figures.

The morale status of each army is monitored twice per turn. There are Turn Sequence Monitor sheets available to help commanders keep track of the battle's progress.

[Turn Sequence chart](#turn-sequence) for the complete play sequence.

title: Movement Rates
content:
In each of the two movement phases of his player turn, the player may either move his figures and vehicles up to the distances given in the movement table, OR figures may perform any one of the actions in the Actions Table.

**Movement Notes:**
- Hover-powered vehicles may pass over scenery up to 1 inch high and treat this as 'Road'
- Anti-grav vehicles may cruise up to 3 inches in height to clear obstacles
- Where troops/vehicles cross more than one terrain type, move at: (terrain type 1 + terrain type 2) ÷ 2
- Any scenery placed on the battlefield should be classified BEFORE play as to how it will affect movement
- Walls over 1 inch will prevent movement across them unless a section is destroyed first

[Movement Table chart](#movement-table) for all rates.

title: Squads and Coherency
content:
In Beamstrike, troops are usually organised into groups of between 3 and 8 troops, called squads or units. This is the basic unit of troop formation.

**Unit/Squad Coherency:** All ground squad members of a unit must stay within 3 inches of at least one other squad member of the same unit. This ensures squad coherency. Distance is measured from any part of one base to any part of another. If the squad moves further apart than this (by movement choice or loss of members), the squad is 'split' and must regroup before it may advance towards an enemy.

**Gang Coherence:** Some units have gang coherence rules. All members must stay within 3 inches of ALL other members of the same unit. Penalty for breaking coherence applies. Civilians without a leader MUST move in this way.

**Independent Units:** Heroes, vehicles, and all heroes may act independently, although bear in mind they may not be rallied if too far from a Leader when morale breaks.

title: Jet Packs
content:
Jet packs are self-powered backpacks that allow troops to make short airborne leaps over obstacles and into battle. A jet pack move may be made in either or both of the player's movement phases.

**Jump Rules:**
- Unarmoured troops and civilians may NOT use jet packs
- A powered jump is usually considered to be half as high as it is long
- For each 1 inch of extra height jumped, 2 inches are lost from the jump length
- Jet packs do have limitations regarding jumps into/out of light, medium, or heavily wooded areas, or jumps from moving vehicles or from building windows
- Stationary or hovering vehicles with open tops may be jumped from at ground attack altitude or landed/ground based

**Jet Pack Accidents:** If troops jump to somewhere they cannot see from the jump start position (line of sight is blocked to the intended landing point), or jump off a building taller than their jump distance, there is a risk of a jet pack accident. For each figure jumping, roll 2D6 and consult the Jetpack Table. If the 2D6 score equals or exceeds the accident score, the figure has had a jump injury/mishap and is removed from play (counts as killed for morale and victory purposes).

See the [Jetpack Table](#jetpack-table) for jump distances and accident scores by armour type.

title: Can You Fire at the Target?
content:
To fire at a target, all of the following must be true:
1. The target must be within the firer's **arc of fire** (90° either side of facing direction)
2. A **line of sight** to the target must exist (unless using indirect fire)
3. The target must be within the weapon's **maximum range**

**Arc of Fire:** Troops may only fire at targets within 90° of either side of their facing direction. Vehicles fire within the arc of their main weapon turret or hull facing.

**Line of Sight:** Required for all direct fire weapons. Cannot have blocks obscuring the line of sight. Indirect fire (grenades, off-table support) does not require line of sight.

**Which Target May Be Chosen?** Normally all weapons in a squad fire aimed at the SAME target (with exceptions). Multiple weapons in PA/AD arm packs must fire at the same target. Vehicle weapons (except those in same turret) may fire at different targets.

title: Hit Modifiers
content:
When firing, roll 2D6 and apply all applicable modifiers. If the final total equals or beats the weapon's base to-hit number for the current range band, the shot hits.

**Key modifiers (see [Hit Modifiers chart](#hit-modifiers) for complete list):**
- Elite firer: +3 | Veteran: +2 | Regular: +0 | Civilian: -2
- Suppressed firer: -2
- Targeter on main weapon: +2
- Target in soft cover: -1, hard cover: -2, reinforced: -3
- Ground vehicle moved last phase: -2
- Deployed support weapon: +2, not deployed: -2

**Critical Hit:** A natural 12 on the 2D6 roll is always a critical hit. Add +2 to the subsequent damage roll.

**ECM Suite:** Negates any Targeter bonus; also applies -2 penalty for vehicle-mounted weapons.

title: Infantry Damage Table
content:
When a figure is hit, roll 1D6 and apply all damage modifiers, then cross-reference the result with the target's armour column in the Infantry Damage Table.

**Damage Results:**
- **NE** — No Effect
- **GH** — Glancing Hit: figure cannot move or fire until after next phase
- **Stun** — No move or attack for 3 turns unless a 6 is rolled on 1D6
- **Kill** — Removed from play (unless medical aid/digimedic available)

**Armour Columns:** UA → FI → LA → PA → PA Shielded → DA → DA Shielded

**Impact Types** (rows, from weakest to strongest): STUN → LOW → STANDARD → HIGH → POWER → TOTAL 1 → TOTAL 2 → TOTAL 3

**Critical Hit bonus:** Natural 12 on the to-hit roll adds +2 to the damage roll.

Note: Figures who have not fired yet but are killed are placed on their sides as a reminder they still can fire (results are applied at end of fire phase).

title: Damage Modifiers
content:
Before consulting the Infantry Damage Table, add or subtract the following modifiers from the 1D6 roll:

- Critical hit (natural 12 to hit): **+2**
- Suitably shielded (active shield vs infantry weapons): **-3**
- Suitably shielded (vs tank gun): **-1**
- Fast infantry in Segregation Era: **-1**
- Hero with Constitution skill: **-2**
- Any Growwlan firearm: **+1**
- Advanced laser pistol/rifle: **+1**
- Heavy bolt pistol: **+1**
- Target is Hibevor/Grey: **+1**
- Target is Thuntra/Orcoid: **-1**
- Kra'vak 'enraged': **-1**
- Kra'vak 'battle rage': **-2**

**Shield going down:** When a shielded figure is hit, the shield goes down on a 6 on 1D6 if a NE hit was received, or 3–6 on 1D6 if a GH hit was received. A downed shield is destroyed for the rest of the battle.

title: Medical Aid and Digimedic
content:
**Medical Aid (Medic skill):** For each medic, a figure within 6 inches may be healed on a 4+ on a 1D6. The figure would normally be removed as killed at the end of the fire phase. Note: in battles with human forces, general convention dictates that Medic figures should not be singled out and shot at if there are other members of the squad in range/visible to the firer. If Medics are shot at, they may make a 'save' roll.

Up to 3 medics per army may be chosen.

**Digimedic (equipment):** If a figure that is hit has a digimedic, roll 1D6. On a 5–6, the hit result is downgraded to the next lower effect:
- Kill → GH (Glancing Hit)
- GH → NE (No Effect)

Digimedics are one-use items — they can only save a figure once.

title: Grenades and Indirect Fire
content:
**Template Weapons:** Some weapons fire using templates:
- **Fire templates** (triangular cone): Point the thin end at the firer, wide end toward the target. All figures within the template are hit.
- **Blast templates** (circular): Centre over the target/impact point. All figures wholly or partially under the template are hit.

**Grenade Throwing:** To determine if a thrown grenade or indirect fire weapon hits, roll 1D6 based on the target's cover (see Grenades and Indirect Fire chart). If the grenade was thrown by a Grenadier, add 1 to the roll.

**Weapon Deployment:** Heavy weapons and squad support weapons must be deployed before firing to gain their full bonus (+2 to hit). Deploying takes 1 full move phase. An undeployed support weapon suffers -2 to hit.

**Snipers** may take 1 move phase to 'deploy', after which they may claim cover bonuses and pick off enemy leaders.

title: Morale and Leadership Points
content:
Each army has a total **Morale Value** calculated before the battle begins. As figures and vehicles are lost, the morale value decreases.

**Morale Points per element:**
- Each trooper = **1 point**
- Each armoured vehicle or hero = **3 points**
- Each nominated building / key defence position / VIP civilian / unmanned vehicle = **5–20 points** (agreed before battle)

**Medics** never count as lost if killed, but do add to an army's morale strength at the beginning of the game (this is because friendly troops are infuriated, not demoralised, if a medic is targeted).

An **armoured vehicle** is only counted as 'lost' for morale purposes if it is 'knocked out' — not if it is only immobilised or has its weapons knocked out or its crew suppressed.

**Morale tracking:** Monitor morale twice per game turn (after each fire phase). Place morale totals on the Turn Monitor Sheet.

title: Shaken and Broken Army
content:
**½ Morale Points = SHAKEN ARMY**
Place a shaken counter next to the army. A shaken army:
- May still fire at eligible targets
- May stand its ground or start to retreat, but **cannot advance**
- Must make a Broken test at the end of each own player turn

**Broken Test:** Throw 2D6. If the score is **7 or more**, the army is 'broken'.

**BROKEN ARMY** — Place broken counter next to army:
- All troops and vehicles must retreat at their maximum movement distance in at least 1 move phase per turn
- Cannot engage any new targets
- Cannot engage those who have fired upon them in the same turn, or those that block off the army's retreat
- Broken troops may not be rallied

A broken army must retreat all troops and vehicles. Exceptions exist for vehicles being repaired and troops rallied by a leader.

title: Squad Suppression
content:
Suppression is caused when a squad is fired upon by:
- A deployed support or heavy weapon
- A deployed sniper
- A weapon from the tank gun chart

**Suppression Test:** Roll 1D6 per squad against the troop roll rating for the majority troop type (see [Troop QR table](#troop-qr)). Apply all suppression modifiers (see [Squad Suppression chart](#squad-suppression)).

**Effect of Suppression:** A suppressed squad MUST move to the NEAREST cover at least 1 level higher than it currently has — unless already in hard/reinforced cover. The suppressed squad may not move toward the enemy. Once in cover, squad may only move if it is to find the nearest better level of cover.

**Removal of Suppression:** The squad must pass a fresh troop roll test (with applicable modifiers) to remove suppression.

Key suppression modifiers:
- Leader in squad: +2
- Leader within 12 inches: +1  
- Squad less than ½ strength: -1
- PA troops: +1 | AD troops: +2
- Beam/flame/sun weapon: -1
- Target in heavy cover: +1 | reinforced: +2

title: Close Combat Overview
content:
Close combat occurs during the Close Combat phase of each player's turn. A figure may make a close combat action when it is within 5cm (approximately 2 inches) of an enemy figure.

**Close Combat Sequence:**
1. Player makes troop rolls for all squads within 5cm of an enemy
2. Successful troops (meeting or beating their troop roll) may make one close combat action
3. Roll 1D6 (if needed) to determine which action is taken, or choose if eligible

See the [CC Actions Table](#cc-actions-table) for the three possible close combat actions (fire close assault weapon, move up to 2 inches, throw a grenade).

**Close assault weapons:** Pistols, auto lasers, shotguns, SMGs, and palm flamers may be fired in close combat.

title: Hand to Hand Combat
content:
Hand to hand (H2H) combat occurs when figures physically engage in melee. Both sides simultaneously roll 1D10 and add all applicable modifiers from the H2H Throw Modifiers chart.

**H2H Resolution:**
- Both combatants roll 1D10 and add all modifiers
- Highest total wins
- In a tie: neither side wins — both may still act in the next phase

**Key H2H modifiers by training:**
- Hero: +3 | Elite: +2 | Veteran: +1 | Regular: +0 | Civilian: -2
- Swordsman skill: +4
- Each extra opponent above 1 (max 4 bonus): +2
- On higher ground: +2

**Weapon modifiers (highest applicable):**
- Lightsword: +8 | Orcoid Warhammer: +6 | Power axe/mono scimitar/Mech claws: +5
- Force sword/power sword/power glove: +3
- Gun butt used: -1

**PA/AD modifiers:** PA infantry: +5 | AD infantry: +6

See the [H2H Throw Modifiers chart](#h2h-throw-modifiers) for the complete list.

title: Cover and Concealment
content:
Cover provides both protection (to-hit penalties to attackers) and concealment (limits how far away a figure can be targeted).

**Cover types and effects on attackers:**
- Soft cover (scrub, light woods, crops): -1 to hit
- Hard cover (buildings, heavy jungle, walls, trenches): -2 to hit
- Reinforced cover (bunkers, armoured vehicles): -3 to hit
- Absolute cover (no line of sight): cannot be targeted

**Concealment Ranges** (beyond this range, the figure cannot be targeted):
- Soft: 10" for figures (7" with scout concealment), 18" for vehicles
- Hard: 6" (4"), 12" vehicles
- Reinforced: 4" (4"), 8" vehicles
- Absolute: 2", 4" vehicles

See the [Cover and Concealment Chart](#cover-concealment) for complete details.

title: Shield Rules
content:
Energy and Projectile shields provide significant protection by modifying the damage roll.

**Shield Types:**
- **ES (Energy Shield):** Protects vs lasers, beam guns, conversion burners, and auto blasters. -3 to damage roll vs infantry weapons, -1 vs vehicle guns and AT.
- **PS (Projectile Shield):** Protects vs bolters, automatics, pistols, ramjet shots, CPP guns (40–150mm), rail gun, vulcan, missiles, and grenades. -3 vs infantry weapons, -1 vs vehicle guns and AT.
- **MULTI Shield:** Acts as either PS or ES. **Vehicle use only.** -2 vs infantry weapons, -1 vs vehicle guns and AT.

**Null-shielded figures** cannot be harmed by the weapon type the shield protects against.

**Downed Shields:**
- Non-penetrating hit received: shield goes down on a **6** (roll 1D6)
- Penetrating hit received: shield goes down on **3–6** (roll 1D6)

Once a shield is down, it is destroyed for the rest of the battle — it cannot be recharged.

title: Vehicle Combat Overview
content:
Vehicle combat uses separate mechanics from infantry combat. The process for resolving a hit on a vehicle is:

1. **To-Hit:** Roll 2D6 + crew troop rating modifier + vehicle to-hit modifiers (size, cover) ≥ weapon's to-hit number
2. **ADS Check (missiles/rockets only):** If target has Automatic Defence System, it may intercept the incoming round (max 2 per turn)
3. **Penetration Roll:** Roll 1D10 (+ critical hit +2 if applicable). Compare weapon impact type to vehicle armour class on the Armour Penetration Chart
4. **If penetrating:** Roll on Vehicle Effect of Penetration Chart (1D10 + TOTAL bonus)
5. **If non-penetrating:** Roll on Non-Penetrating Damage chart (if weapon is powerful enough)

**Vehicle Classes:**
- Class 0–1: Soft Skins (unmanned vehicles, aircraft) — SS
- Class 2: Light vehicles (APC, scout car, aircraft, light mecha) — VL-VA
- Class 3: Light tanks, medium mecha — L-VA
- Class 4: Medium tanks, heavy walker mecha — M-VA
- Class 5: Heavy tanks — H-VA
- Class 6: Super heavy tanks (composite armour) — VH-VA

title: Materials Required
content:
To play Beamstrike you will need:

- **Miniatures:** Infantry figures (15mm or 25mm scale recommended), vehicles, and terrain pieces to represent the battlefield
- **Dice:** 2D6 (two six-sided dice) for to-hit rolls, 1D6 for damage and other checks, 1D10 for hand-to-hand combat
- **Measuring device:** A ruler or tape measure in inches
- **Tokens/counters:** To track suppression, shaken/broken status, deployed weapons, and casualty markers
- **Army lists:** Agreed-upon points limit and army rosters
- **Turn Monitor Sheet:** Recommended to track morale totals each turn

All distances in Beamstrike are measured in **inches**. When measuring, distances are measured from the nearest point of one figure's base to the nearest point of the target's base.

title: Game Turn Structure
content:
A game of Beamstrike is divided into **GAME TURNS**. Each game turn consists of **2 PLAYER TURNS** — one for each player.

**Each Player Turn contains:**
1. **First Movement Phase** — move troops and vehicles
2. **First Fire Phase** — both players fire simultaneously
3. **Close Combat Phase** — resolve hand-to-hand combat
4. **Morale Check** — check army morale state
5. **Second Movement Phase** — further movement
6. **Second Fire Phase** — both players fire simultaneously
7. **Morale Check** — check army morale state again

**Key rule:** Casualties are only removed *after* both players have fired in a fire phase, not during. Figures killed but not yet removed may still fire in that phase — lay them on their sides to indicate this.

Players alternate who takes their turn first each game turn (or decide by dice roll at the start).

title: Actions Table
content:
Instead of moving, a figure or squad may perform **one action** from the Actions Table per movement phase. Actions represent activities that take the entire movement phase to complete.

**Common actions available:**
- **Deploy/Redeploy weapon** — Set up or pack away a support or heavy weapon. Deployed weapons gain +2 to hit; undeployed suffer -2.
- **Entrench** — Dig in to gain cover benefits. Requires a full movement phase.
- **Laser paint target** — Mark a target for off-table fire support (requires Scout or leader skill).
- **Board/disembark vehicle** — Enter or exit a vehicle or Q-travel pod.
- **Reload** — Reload a weapon that requires it (e.g. single-shot AT weapons).
- **Medical treatment** — Medic uses their skill to heal a nearby figure.
- **Repair vehicle** — Engineer/mechanic attempts to repair a damaged vehicle system.

Figures that perform an action in a movement phase **may not move** in that phase. They may still fire normally in a subsequent fire phase unless the action specifically states otherwise.

title: Ground Vehicle Manoeuvres
content:
Ground vehicles follow the standard movement rates from the Movement Table but have additional manoeuvre rules.

**Turning:** Vehicles may turn up to 45° for free during movement. Each additional 45° of turn costs 1 inch of movement.

**Reverse:** Vehicles may move in reverse at half their normal movement rate.

**Terrain effects on vehicles:**
- Road: full movement rate
- Open ground: full movement rate
- Light cover (scrub, crops): half movement
- Heavy cover (woods, rubble): quarter movement or impassable depending on vehicle size
- Walls/obstacles up to 1 inch: tracked/wheeled vehicles may attempt to cross (roll required for larger obstacles)

**Tank shock:** A vehicle that moves into contact with infantry may cause a morale test. Infantry figures contacted must pass a troop roll or be suppressed.

**Ramming:** Vehicles may attempt to ram other vehicles. Both vehicles take damage based on their relative armour classes.

**Hovering vehicles** treat all terrain up to 1 inch high as road. Anti-grav vehicles may climb up to 3 inches to clear obstacles.

title: Aircraft
content:
Aircraft operate under special movement rules due to their speed and altitude.

**Aircraft Movement:**
- Aircraft move in a straight line at the start of their movement phase
- Aircraft must move at least their minimum speed each phase or stall
- Aircraft may strafe targets along their flight path
- Aircraft may not hover unless they are VTOL (no minimum speed requirement)

**Altitude:**
- Aircraft fly at low (below 6"), medium (6"–18"), or high (above 18") altitude
- Altitude affects what weapons can target the aircraft and what the aircraft can target
- Anti-aircraft weapons have altitude restrictions in their stats

**Anti-aircraft fire:**
- Standard infantry and vehicle weapons may fire at aircraft at -4 to hit (low altitude), -6 (medium), or cannot fire (high)
- AA-rated weapons fire at normal modifiers

**Aircraft as reinforcements:** Aircraft may be used to deliver troops (see Reinforcements — Point drop-ship) or as fire support (off-table fire support strafing run).

title: Q-Travel Pods
content:
Q-Travel Pods are quantum teleportation devices placed on the battlefield that allow troops to rapidly redeploy.

**Placement:** Placed anywhere on the battlefield before troops are deployed. Can be destroyed by enemy troops.

**Capacity:** Each Q-travel pod can transport up to **5 figures of any troop class** or a light vehicle (up to hull class 2), in each movement phase.

**Entering and Exiting:**
- The bay can only be used in **one move phase per player turn** (either entering or exiting, not both)
- Troops move freely from 'reinforcement' status into any Q-pod in any movement phase — no dice roll needed
- Troops may fire from the Q-pod in the fire phase and move off the pod in the next friendly movement phase
- Troops may move from one pod to any other on the table in the same movement phase

**Q-pod Destruction:**
- Destroyed on any 'KILL' result from ranged fire
- Counts as a stationary assault dreadnought armoured troop for armour purposes when being targeted
- Glancing hit results have no effect
- May be inactivated by any trooper in base-to-base contact armed with a power or force weapon

**Points cost:** 30 points per pod (includes orbital craft support and control mechanisms).

**Evacuation scenarios:** Troops move into a Q-pod, and in the next move phase are teleported to a waiting starship.

title: Arcs of Fire
content:
Every figure and vehicle has a defined arc within which they may fire.

**Infantry Arc of Fire:**
- Troops may fire at any target within **90° on either side** of their facing direction (180° total arc)
- Figures may be turned to face any direction at the start of their fire phase

**Vehicle Arcs of Fire:**
- **Hull-mounted weapons:** fire within a 45° arc of the vehicle hull facing
- **Turret-mounted weapons:** fire within a 360° arc (full rotation)
- **Pintle/top-mounted weapons:** fire within a 180° arc
- Vehicle hull may fire at a different target from the turret (they have separate arcs)

**Line of Sight Requirements:**
- A clear, unobstructed line of sight must exist from firer to target
- Sight lines are drawn from the firer's eyes/weapon barrel to any part of the target figure
- If at least 50% of the target is visible, it may be fired upon
- Figures in 'absolute cover' (fully behind terrain) cannot be targeted at all

**Hull-down position:** A vehicle may be placed partially behind cover so only its turret is visible. This counts as reinforced cover against most weapon types.

title: Target Selection
content:
Rules for choosing which targets may be engaged.

**Standard targeting rules:**
- A squad **without a leader** must fire at the **nearest visible enemy** in arc
- A squad **with a leader** (Leadership skill) may choose any eligible target in arc and range
- All figures in a squad must fire at the **same target** (with exceptions)

**Exceptions — figures that may fire independently:**
- Heroes always fire independently
- PA/AD troops with separate weapon packs may fire different packs at different targets
- Vehicle weapons in different turrets/mounts may fire at different targets

**Target priority:**
- If multiple enemy figures are equidistant and are the nearest visible enemy, the firing player may choose which to target
- A squad that has been fired upon in the same phase may fire back at any visible attacker regardless of distance

**Snipers:** A deployed sniper ignores normal targeting rules. They may specifically target enemy leader/hero figures ('picking off' key enemy individuals), even if those figures are not the nearest visible enemy.

title: Weapon Use
content:
Rules governing how weapons are used in the fire phase.

**Shots per Phase:** Most infantry weapons fire once per phase. Some weapons (e.g. HMG, Vulcan) fire multiple times — the number is indicated in the weapon stats.

**Burst Fire:** Automatic weapons with multiple shots-per-phase roll to-hit once and apply all shots to the same target. Each shot that hits rolls damage separately.

**Snap Fire:** Figures that moved in the same phase they are firing suffer -2 to hit.

**Overwatch:** A figure or vehicle that does not fire in a phase may be placed on Overwatch. It may fire at any enemy moving within its arc of fire before that enemy reaches its intended destination. An Overwatch shot counts as that figure's fire for the current fire phase.

**Weapon Deployment:**
- Support weapons and heavy weapons must be deployed before firing at full effectiveness
- Deploying takes one full movement phase (the figure performs 'deploy weapon' as its action)
- Deployed: +2 to hit. Not deployed: -2 to hit
- A sniper must also deploy to claim full concealment and sniper bonuses

**Reload:** Some weapons (single-shot AT, missile launchers) must be reloaded after firing. Reloading counts as the figure's action for that movement phase.

title: Template Weapons
content:
Some weapons use physical templates to determine which figures are affected.

**Fire Templates (triangular cone):**
- Used by flamers, conversion burners, palm flamers, and similar area-effect weapons
- Available in 5 sizes (1 = short/pistol, 2 = rifle, 3 = support, 4–5 = heavy burst)
- The narrow end is placed at the weapon barrel; the wide end points toward the target
- **All figures wholly or partially within the template are hit automatically** — no to-hit roll needed
- Both friendly and enemy figures in the template are hit
- Hit figures roll on the Infantry Damage Table using the weapon's impact type

**Blast Templates (circular):**
- Used by grenades, mortars, off-table artillery, and some support weapons
- Available in 4 sizes
- Centre the template over the target point (or where the grenade/shell lands)
- **All figures wholly or partially under the template are hit**
- To determine if a grenade lands on target, roll 1D6 (see Grenades and Indirect Fire chart)
- Scatter: if a grenade misses, determine scatter direction and distance on a D6

**Suppressions from templates:** Flame and beam templates cause additional -1 to suppression rolls.

title: Off-Table Fire Support
content:
Off-table fire support represents artillery, air strikes, orbital bombardment, and similar assets called in from beyond the battlefield.

**Calling in Support:**
- To call in off-table fire support, a figure with **Leader or Scout skill** must use their action to 'laser paint' (designate) the target
- Roll 1D6: on a **4–6** the support is successfully called in (leader: 4+, scout with laser painter: 4+, other: no call allowed)
- A **Laser Painter** device is required to call in fire support (counts as an equipment item)

**Arrival:**
- Support arrives in the **next friendly fire phase** after being called
- Successful support is placed within 2" of the designated target area
- Roll for scatter if appropriate to the weapon type

**Types of off-table support:**
- Artillery barrage: large blast template, multiple turns of bombardment
- Air strike/strafing run: fire template along a specified flight path
- Orbital strike: very large blast template, extremely powerful

**Suppression rule:** All figures within/near an off-table support impact suffer suppression tests regardless of troop type (exception: robots/androids and fanatic troops).

**Cost:** Off-table support assets are purchased in the army list and count toward the points limit.

title: CC Actions Table
content:
During the Close Combat phase, figures within 5cm (approximately 2") of an enemy may take one close combat action. The figure must pass its troop roll first.

**The three possible close combat actions:**

| Roll (1D6) | Action |
|---|---|
| 1–2 | **Fire close assault weapon** — Fire a pistol, auto laser, shotgun, SMG, or palm flamer at an enemy in base contact. Uses standard ranged combat rules at point-blank range. |
| 3–4 | **Advance up to 2 inches** — Move toward the nearest enemy to initiate hand-to-hand contact, or to improve position. |
| 5–6 | **Throw a grenade** — Throw a grenade at a nearby enemy group (maximum 6" range in close combat). |

**Notes:**
- Players with eligible figures may choose their action *or* roll 1D6 if they prefer random resolution
- Figures already in base contact with an enemy may immediately resolve Hand-to-Hand combat instead of rolling for an action
- Close assault weapons that may be fired in close combat: pistols, palm flamers, auto laser, shotgun, SMG — NOT main rifles or support weapons

title: Vehicle Types and Classes
content:
Vehicles in Beamstrike are classified by their hull class (armour rating) and hull type (size and role).

**Hull Classes (Armour Rating):**
| Class | Description | Abbreviation |
|---|---|---|
| 0 | Soft skin — unarmoured | SS |
| 1 | Very light armour (bicycles, atv, open-top) | SS |
| 2 | Light armoured (APC, scout car, light mecha) | VL-VA |
| 3 | Light tank, medium mecha | L-VA |
| 4 | Medium tank, heavy walker mecha | M-VA |
| 5 | Heavy tank | H-VA |
| 6 | Super heavy tank — composite armour | VH-VA |

**Hull Types (affect capacity and maneuvrability):**
- APC, Scout Car, Bike/Trike, Transport
- Light Mecha, Medium Mecha, Heavy Mecha
- Light Walker, Medium Walker, Heavy Walker
- Light Tank, Medium Tank, Heavy Tank, Super Heavy Tank
- Aircraft, GMC (Grav Military Carrier)

**Vehicle Morale Value:** All armoured vehicles (hull class 2+) count as **3 morale points** each when lost.

**Vehicle Crews:** Vehicles have a crew (typically 1–4 figures). Crew training class affects to-hit rolls. Crew may be killed/suppressed by penetrating hits.

title: Vehicle Hit Sequence
content:
When a vehicle is hit, follow this sequence:

**Step 1 — ADS (Automatic Defence System):**
If the vehicle has an ADS and the incoming weapon is a missile or rocket, roll 1D10. On a **6–10**, the ADS destroys the incoming round. Maximum 2 rounds intercepted per turn.

**Step 2 — Armour Penetration Roll:**
Roll 1D10 and compare vs the weapon's impact type on the Armour Penetration Chart, cross-referenced with the vehicle's hull class. Results:
- **No Effect** — weapon cannot penetrate this armour class
- **Non-penetrating** — some effect but armour holds
- **Penetrating** — full penetration, roll on effects table
- **Critical** — natural 10 on D10 at penetrating result: +2 to effects table roll

**Step 3 — Vehicle Hit Effects (if penetrating):**
Roll 1D10 on the Vehicle Effect of Penetration Chart. Results range from minor crew suppression through weapon damage, fire, immobilisation, to total destruction.

**Step 4 — Non-Penetrating Damage (if non-penetrating):**
If the weapon was powerful enough to cause non-penetrating effects, roll on the Non-Penetrating Damage Chart.

title: Armour Penetration
content:
The Armour Penetration Chart determines whether a weapon can penetrate a vehicle's armour.

**Penetration Roll:** Roll 1D10. Add +2 for a critical hit (natural 12 on the to-hit roll).

**Cross-reference the weapon's impact type against the vehicle's hull class:**

| Impact | Class 0–1 | Class 2 | Class 3 | Class 4 | Class 5 | Class 6 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| LOW | P | NP | NE | NE | NE | NE |
| STANDARD | P | P | NP | NE | NE | NE |
| HIGH | P | P | P | NP | NE | NE |
| POWER | P | P | P | P | NP | NE |
| TOTAL 1 | P | P | P | P | P | NP |
| TOTAL 2/3 | P | P | P | P | P | P |

P = Penetrating, NP = Non-Penetrating, NE = No Effect

**For penetrating results:** Roll 1D10 on the Vehicle Effect of Penetration Chart.
**For non-penetrating results:** Roll 1D10 on the Non-Penetrating Damage Chart (only if the weapon can cause NP effects against this armour class).

See the [Armour Penetration Chart](#armour-penetration-chart) for complete details.

title: Vehicle Hit Effects
content:
When a vehicle is penetrated, roll 1D10 on the Vehicle Effect of Penetration Chart (add +2 for TOTAL impact weapons).

**Effect Results (1D10 + modifiers):**

| Roll | Result |
|---|---|
| 1–2 | **Crew Suppressed** — vehicle may not move or fire for 1 full turn |
| 3–4 | **Weapon Destroyed** — one vehicle weapon permanently destroyed (attacker chooses) |
| 5–6 | **Immobilised** — vehicle cannot move but may still fire |
| 7–8 | **Catastrophic Hit** — vehicle destroyed, crew killed |
| 9–10 | **Explodes** — catastrophic destruction, figures within 3" are hit by blast |
| 11+ | **Spectacular Explosion** — as Explodes but 6" radius, with additional suppression effect |

**Notes:**
- An immobilised vehicle that is hit again and immobilised is counted as destroyed
- A vehicle on fire: roll 1D6 at the start of each turn; on a 1–3 it is destroyed
- Crew suppressed vehicles: crew may attempt to un-suppress at start of their next turn (troop roll)

See the [Vehicle Effects Chart](#vehicle-effects-chart) for full results.

title: Non-Penetrating Hits
content:
Some weapons are powerful enough to cause non-penetrating damage even when they cannot fully penetrate a vehicle's armour.

**Non-Penetrating Effects:** When a non-penetrating hit occurs (weapon impact type one step below what's needed to penetrate) and the weapon has NP capability against this hull class, roll 1D6:

| Roll | Result |
|---|---|
| 1–3 | **No Effect** — weapon did not have enough force |
| 4–5 | **Crew Suppressed** — crew shaken but vehicle intact; cannot move or fire for 1 phase |
| 6 | **Weapon Knocked Out** — one weapons system temporarily inoperative; roll 1D6 each turn, on 5–6 it is repaired |

**Glancing hits on Q-Travel Pods and shields:** Glancing hits from infantry weapons have no effect on Q-travel pods. Shields — a non-penetrating hit causes shield to go down on roll of 6 on 1D6.

**Soft skin vehicles (class 0–1):** These vehicles have no armour penetration step — any hit that would wound infantry is resolved directly on the crew as though they were infantry figures.

title: Vehicle To-Hit Modifiers
content:
Modifiers that apply when firing at vehicles with ranged weapons.

**Base roll:** 2D6 plus applicable modifiers must equal or exceed the weapon's to-hit number for the range band.

**Size modifiers (vehicle is the target):**
| Vehicle Size | Modifier |
|---|---|
| Very small (bike, trike) | -2 |
| Small (scout car, light mecha) | +0 |
| Medium (APC, light tank) | +1 |
| Large (medium tank, heavy mecha) | +2 |
| Very large (heavy/super heavy tank) | +3 |

**Other to-hit modifiers vs vehicles:**
- Vehicle moved last phase: attacker **+1** (easier target)
- Vehicle stationary/hull-down: attacker **-2**
- Soft cover (smoke, scrub): **-1**
- Firing crew training: Elite +3, Veteran +2, Regular +0, Civilian -2
- Targeter fitted: **+2**
- ECM suite on target: **-2** (negates targeter bonus)
- Snap fire (vehicle moved before firing): **-2**

See the [Vehicle To-Hit Modifiers Chart](#vehicle-to-hit-modifiers-chart) for a complete reference.

title: Alien Races Overview
content:
The universe of Beamstrike is populated by numerous alien races, each with distinctive combat capabilities, armour types, and special rules.

**Main Alien Races in ERA 1:**
- **Spug** — Insectoid warriors. Drone troops bred for combat. Use pheromone-based command structure. Omega/Theta drones with specialised roles. May use advanced force swords and power claws.
- **Thuntra** — Large, powerful reptilian warriors with natural high toughness (-1 to damage rolls). May use heavy power axes. Very strong in close combat.
- **Hibevor** — Unique alien biology makes them more vulnerable (+1 to damage rolls) but their pheromone-based organisation gives tactical advantages.
- **Grey** — Technologically advanced small grey aliens. Use paralysis rods in melee. Known for abduction scenarios.
- **Ferrapur** — Advanced alien warriors, may use advanced force swords.
- **Centaling** — Alien race with access to advanced melee weapons.
- **Orcoid** — Large, brutish warriors with massive warhammers (+6 in H2H).
- **Kra'vak** — Fearsome alien warriors with battle rage ability (-2 to damage when enraged).
- **Growwlan** — Alien race whose firearms cause +1 to damage rolls.

**Special Race Rules:** Each alien race has unique weapon access restrictions, morale quirks, and special abilities defined in the race-specific sections.

title: Alien Weapons
content:
Many alien races have access to unique weapons not available to human factions. These weapons are marked with race restrictions in the weapon lists.

**Notable alien-exclusive weapons:**
- **Advanced Force Sword (AFS):** H2H bonus +4, ONLY Ferrapur, Centaling, and Spug races may use. 3 pts.
- **Imperial Warhammer (IWH):** H2H +4, 2-handed. Only Imperial forces.
- **Imperial Warsword (IWS):** H2H +4, impact POWER, 2-handed. Only Imperial forces.
- **Power Claw (PCL):** H2H +5, HIGH impact. SPUG Omega and Theta drone leaders, Spugnaught exo-suit only.
- **Paralysis Rod (PAR):** H2H +3, STUN impact. Grey race only. If Grey wins combat, enemy is paralysed and beamed up.
- **Heavy Power Axe (HPA):** H2H +5, POWER impact. Thuntra races only. Requires Swordsman skill.
- **Pirate Multiblades (PMB):** H2H +3, HIGH impact. Requires Swordsman skill. May throw 2D10 in combat taking highest roll. Pirates/Merchant Barons only.
- **Crimson Rebel Power Glaive (CPG):** H2H +5, POWER, 2-handed. Only Crimson Rebel forces.

**AM-Beamer:** Alien Marines Beamer — a powerful energy weapon unique to alien marine factions.

All alien weapons are listed in the weapons tables with their `racesAllowed` field specifying which races may use them.

title: Alien Figure Movement
content:
Most alien figures follow standard movement rules, but several races have special movement capabilities.

**Standard movement:** Alien infantry use the same movement table as human infantry, adjusted for their armour type (UA, FI, LA, PA, AD).

**Special alien movement rules:**
- **Spug drones:** Certain Spug drone types have enhanced movement capabilities due to their insectoid physiology. Some models may climb vertical surfaces.
- **Hibevor:** Standard infantry movement rates but with special squad coherency rules based on pheromone range.
- **Thuntra:** Slightly slower than average due to large body size despite their power, but gain bonuses in difficult terrain.
- **Grey:** Grey figures are typically UA or FI class and move at standard rates. However, Grey mothership teleportation gives them exceptional strategic mobility.

**Alien vehicles:** Alien factions may field vehicles with unique hull types. These follow standard vehicle movement rules with any race-specific exceptions noted in their army list entries.

**Flying/Levitating aliens:** Some alien types have innate flight or levitation abilities that overrule standard movement rules. These are treated as having built-in jet packs that cannot malfunction.

Consult the specific alien faction section and the [Aliens Troop sheet](#aliens-troops) for each race's exact movement stats.

title: Leadership and Rallying Troops
content:
Each army must purchase **at least one figure with Leadership skill** to act as the duty officer. Players often purchase one leader per squad for maximum effect.

**Rallying broken troops:**
When an army is 'broken' and starts to retreat, all leaders present may attempt to rally remaining troops around them.

**Rally procedure:**
- For each leader, roll **1D6** — the score is the number of troops the leader has rallied who will stay with him
- **Heroes with Leadership skill** may roll **2D6**, taking the highest single figure (not adding the dice together)
- Troops must be **within 8 inches** of the leader when the rally roll is made, or they are too far away to be rallied
- Rallied troops may fire and move as normal, and **do not have to retreat** with the rest of the army
- Rallied troops now count as a **new unit/squad** for coherency purposes, even though they may have come from different original infantry elements

**Leader placement:**
Leader figures may be assigned to each squad for greatest effect, or a separate 'command' squad may be created containing the leader figure.

See also: [Suppression modifiers](#morale-suppression) — a leader in squad gives +2 to suppression roll; a leader within 12" gives +1.

title: Fanatic Troops and Robots
content:
**Fanatic troops and robots** are immune to normal morale rules.

**Fanatic Troops:**
Morale rules do not apply to figures with the **Fanatic skill**. Any fanatic human troops which were **not fired upon in the last fire phase** must move straight towards the nearest enemy unless:
- They are in a coherent squad containing a leader, OR
- Another leader or hero figure is within **8 inches**

The advance must be directly towards the **nearest visible enemy**. If no enemy is visible, fanatic troops advance towards the **enemy table edge**.

Fanatic troops may NOT retreat when the army's morale is broken — they fight on regardless.

**Robots:**
Robots are also immune to morale rules. They follow the same basic behaviour pattern as fanatics but are additionally subject to the special robot rules (see Androids and Robots in Battle).

**Fanatic skill cost:** 3 points per figure. Generally restricted to Rebel troops, some Alien races, and war droids.

**Note:** An army composed entirely of fanatics/robots has no need for morale tracking, but still requires the mandatory minimum of one leader figure for army construction.

title: Reinforcement Methods
content:
Reinforcements are extra units held off-table until called. They are not placed at the start of the game, but held off the table until they are called.

**Points discount:** Units allocated as reinforcements only cost **80% of the points value** of regular units (e.g. a 100-point tank costs 80 points as a reinforcement).

**Three methods to bring on reinforcements:**

**1. Q-Travel Pod (30 points per pod)**
Placed anywhere on the battlefield before troops deploy. Troops freely move from reinforcement status into any Q-pod — no dice roll needed. See [Q-Travel Pods](#movement-qtravelpods) for full rules.

**2. Ground Based (no extra points cost)**
Reinforcing units enter from the friendly table edge.
- Roll 1D6 during each friendly movement phase for each squad/vehicle/independent figure
- On a **4–6**, the unit arrives (a leader makes the call-in roll instead of random)
- Successfully called units are placed within **2 inches** of the friendly table edge
- They may not move further in that movement phase; thereafter they move and fire normally

**3. Point Drop-Ship (20 points per ship)**
- A hull class 5 drop ship with capacity for **24 FI troops**, or a hull class 2 or 3 vehicle, or 2 hull class 1 vehicles, or 3 hull class 0 vehicles
- The drop ship only drops troops anywhere on the table then immediately leaves
- Roll 1D6 each friendly movement phase: **4–6** the ship arrives (leader makes the roll)
- A Laser Painter is NOT needed to call a drop-ship
- Successfully called: placed in a 'landed' position. Next friendly move phase troops disembark within 1" of the ship
- The ship then takes off in the next move phase. During this window the enemy may fire on the landed ship (classed as airborne then landed)
- Drop ships that have just left the battlefield may be called again in the next friendly movement phase

**Evacuation scenarios:** Troops move into a Q-pod or drop-ship, and in the next move phase are transported safely to a waiting starship.

title: Reinforcements and Morale
content:
Reinforcement units interact with morale rules in specific ways.

**Only units in play count toward morale.** Reinforcements off-table do not count toward either army's morale total until they physically arrive on the battlefield.

**Arriving reinforcements:** When a reinforcement unit successfully enters the table, its morale value is immediately added to the army's current morale total. This can have significant effects:
- If an army is **'shaken'** you may still attempt to bring on reinforcements. A successful arrival may push the morale total back above the half-value threshold, meaning the army is **no longer shaken**
- If an army is **'broken'**, adding reinforcements does not alter the broken status of the existing force — they must still retreat as per the core morale rules
- However, any reinforcements called in the next turn after an army becomes broken may be added to any **'rallied' troops** (see Leadership and Rallying Troops), if they appear on the table edge within **8 inches** of a leader who has just rallied

**Unrallied reinforcements:** Reinforcements that have not been rallied, or those still off-table when the army breaks, are considered **lost** after the army is broken.

**Existing troops on table:** Note that existing troops can also be moved around the battlefield in ground-based vehicles or in drop-ship-type aircraft that have been purchased for continual use (rather than point drop-ships).

title: Androids and Robots Rules
content:
The use of androids and robots on the future battlefield is commonplace. Often built stronger than mere flesh and hard-wired to ignore suppression and morale, they make excellent warriors. Variants include massive walking artillery support bots, stealthy assassin droids, maintenance robots, and technical robots.

**Ranged combat:** Robots and androids are moved and shoot in the same way as living troops. Ranged combat is treated normally if the robot or android is fitted with firearms.

**RULES SPECIFIC TO ROBOTS:**
- **-2 penalty** to Hand to Hand combat roll (often have inferior close combat capability)
- Each time a robot receives a **glancing hit** (hit but not killed), roll 1D6. On a **5 or 6**, the robot has a **malfunction** — it may not move or fire for 1D3 turns
- **May not** call off-table fire support or reinforcements
- Robots **may not** be classed as Heroes
- Robots do not count as 'fanatics' but ARE immune to morale rules (they cannot be suppressed or broken)
- Robots do not benefit from Leadership bonuses

**RULES SPECIFIC TO ANDROIDS:**
- **-2 penalty** to Hand to Hand combat roll
- Androids are more sophisticated than robots and may optionally be granted skills (at normal cost)
- Androids ARE immune to morale and suppression rules
- Unlike robots, androids do not suffer from the malfunction rule on glancing hits — they are more resilient
- Androids may be purchased as Hero-class figures if their points reflect this

**Fanatic robots and androids:** War droids with the Fanatic skill advance toward the nearest enemy when not fired upon — see [Fanatic Troops](#leadership-fanatic).
